text:
# Concrete Works

## Planning and Sequencing

All concrete pours must be planned in advance and recorded on the pour card system. The pour card records the element to be cast, the concrete mix reference, the planned volume, and the required checks before, during, and after the pour. No pour may begin until the pour card has been signed by the section engineer confirming that formwork, reinforcement, and embedded items have been checked against the drawings.

Concrete deliveries must be

against the drawings.

Concrete deliveries must be scheduled so that each load is placed within its workability window. As a general rule on this project, concrete must be discharged within 90 minutes of batching unless the mix design includes a retarder and the supplier confirms an extended window on the delivery ticket.

## Formwork

Formwork must be designed for the full pressure of fresh concrete, including the effects of pour rate, concrete temperature, and vibration. The temporary works

te temperature, and vibration. The temporary works coordinator must approve all formwork designs and any departures from them.

- Erect formwork on firm, level bearings; check props for plumb and correct pitch.
- Seal joints to prevent grout loss, which weakens edges and creates honeycombing.
- Apply release agent thinly and evenly before fixing reinforcement, not after.
- Provide safe access platforms for placing and vibrating concrete; do not climb on formwork.

Formwork must be inspected and

climb on formwork.

Formwork must be inspected and signed off immediately before the pour. Striking times must follow the striking schedule, which is based on concrete strength gain rather than fixed ages; in cold weather, striking must be delayed until maturity calculations or cube results confirm adequate strength. Props supporting soffits must not be removed early to recover materials.

## Reinforcement

Reinforcement must be fixed as shown on the bending schedules and placement drawings,

n on the bending schedules and placement drawings, with the correct cover maintained using purpose-made spacers. Cover blocks made from broken concrete or timber offcuts are not permitted. Projecting starter bars must be protected with proprietary caps to prevent impalement injuries wherever a person could fall onto them.

## Placing and Compaction

Concrete must be placed as close as possible to its final position and must not be moved laterally by vibration, which causes segregation. The

erally by vibration, which causes segregation. The maximum free-fall height for concrete is 1.5 metres; use chutes, tremie pipes, or pump lines for deeper drops.

Compact concrete with internal pokers in layers not exceeding 500 mm. Insert the poker vertically at regular spacings, penetrate the layer below by about 100 mm to knit the layers together, and withdraw it slowly. Over-vibration brings excess fines and water to the surface; under-vibration leaves honeycombing around reinforcement and

ation leaves honeycombing around reinforcement and in corners.

Workers handling fresh concrete must wear waterproof gloves, long sleeves, and waterproof boots. Wet cement is corrosive to skin and can cause serious chemical burns; any skin contact must be washed off immediately with clean water, and contaminated clothing changed.

## Curing

Curing protects concrete from early drying and temperature extremes so that it gains its design strength. Begin curing as soon as the surface can accept it

Begin curing as soon as the surface can accept it without damage.

- For slabs, apply curing compound in an even coat, or cover with polythene sheeting lapped and weighted at the edges.
- Keep surfaces continuously damp where water curing is specified; intermittent wetting causes surface crazing.
- Maintain curing for at least seven days for ordinary structural mixes, or as specified for special mixes.

## Cold Weather Concreting

When the air temperature is at or below 5 degrees Celsius and

r temperature is at or below 5 degrees Celsius and falling, cold weather precautions apply. Concrete must not be placed against frozen ground, ice, or frost-covered reinforcement. The temperature of the concrete at delivery must be at least 5 degrees Celsius, and the placed concrete must be kept above 5 degrees Celsius until it reaches 5 N/mm² strength, using insulated blankets, enclosures, or heated aggregates as necessary. Accelerating admixtures may be used only with the approval of the

mixtures may be used only with the approval of the section engineer, and calcium chloride is not permitted in reinforced concrete.

---

# Electrical Safety on Site

## Temporary Site Electrics

Temporary electrical installations on this site are designed, installed, and maintained by the appointed electrical contractor. No other trade may alter distribution boards, transformers, or fixed wiring. Requests for additional supplies must be made through the site office.

Portable tools on site operate at 110 volts centre-tapped-to-earth, supplied through yellow transformers and connectors, so that the maximum voltage to earth is

onnectors, so that the maximum voltage to earth is limited to 55 volts. Battery-powered tools are preferred wherever practicable and are mandatory in damp or confined locations unless a specific risk assessment approves otherwise. Mains-voltage 230-volt equipment is not permitted in the general working area; it may be used only in the site offices and welfare units, protected by 30 mA RCDs.

Generators must be positioned in ventilated outdoor locations, earthed in accordance with the electrical

cations, earthed in accordance with the electrical contractor's arrangements, and refuelled only when shut down and cool.

## Cable Management

Trailing cables are a leading cause of trips and of damage-related electrical faults on construction sites.

- Route cables at high level on proprietary hooks wherever possible, keeping them clear of walkways, doorways, and vehicle routes.
- Where cables must cross a walkway or roadway at ground level, protect them with cable ramps rated for the traffic

rotect them with cable ramps rated for the traffic expected.
- Keep connectors out of standing water and off wet ground; suspend them or place them on stands.
- Uncoil extension leads fully before use to prevent overheating.
- Inspect cables daily for cuts, abrasion, and damaged plugs, and remove damaged items from service immediately.

Buried services must be located, marked, and where necessary exposed by hand digging before any excavation begins; see the excavation guidance document for the

gins; see the excavation guidance document for the full permit-to-dig procedure.

## Inspection and Testing Intervals

All portable and transportable electrical equipment used on site must carry a current test label. The combined inspection and test intervals for this project are:

- 110-volt portable tools and leads: every 3 months.
- 230-volt equipment in site offices: every 12 months.
- RCDs: user check with the test button before each use, plus formal test every 3 months.
- Fixed temporary

plus formal test every 3 months.
- Fixed temporary installation: inspected and tested every 3 months by the electrical contractor.

Equipment without a valid test label, or with an expired label, must be taken out of use and returned to the tool store. User checks before each use remain essential regardless of the test label: look for damaged casings, bent pins, loose cable grips, and signs of overheating.

## Lockout and Isolation

Work on any electrical circuit or on plant with stored energy

electrical circuit or on plant with stored energy requires isolation under the site lockout-tagout procedure.

1. Identify all energy sources feeding the equipment, including secondary supplies and control circuits.
2. Isolate at the identified points and lock each isolator with a personal padlock; every worker on the task fits their own lock to the multi-lock hasp.
3. Fit a tag stating the work in progress, the name of the person who applied the lock, and the date.
4. Prove the circuit dead

the lock, and the date.
4. Prove the circuit dead at the point of work with a voltage indicator, proving the indicator on a known live source or proving unit before and after.
5. Only the person who fitted a lock may remove it. Locks may be removed by the site manager only under the documented emergency lock-removal procedure.

Live working is not permitted on this site. Fault-finding that requires energised equipment must be referred to the electrical contractor and carried out under their

electrical contractor and carried out under their safe system of work.

## Overhead Lines and Plant

Where overhead power lines cross or border the site, goalposts and barriers define the safe passage routes and exclusion zones. Cranes, excavators, tipper bodies, and ladders must never enter the exclusion zone. If any plant or load contacts an overhead line, the operator must stay in the cab, warn others to keep clear, and contact the site office; leave the cab by jumping clear, without

te office; leave the cab by jumping clear, without touching the machine and the ground at the same time, only if fire makes staying impossible.

---

# Excavation and Groundworks

## Permit to Dig

No excavation, including hand digging, post holes, and soil nails, may start without a permit to dig issued by the section engineer. The permit confirms that buried services drawings have been reviewed, that a cable avoidance tool (CAT) and signal generator survey has been completed and marked on the ground, and that trial holes have been dug by hand where services are indicated. Permits are valid for the area, depth, and duration stated on them

d for the area, depth, and duration stated on them and must be re-validated if conditions change.

Mechanical excavation is not permitted within 500 mm of a marked service; the final approach to any service must be by hand digging with insulated tools or by vacuum excavation. Any service found that is not on the drawings must be treated as live, work stopped, and the section engineer informed.

## Trench Support and Battering

The decision on how to support an excavation rests with the

ion on how to support an excavation rests with the temporary works coordinator and depends on soil type, depth, groundwater, surcharge from plant and spoil, and the duration the excavation will stay open. No one may enter an unsupported excavation deeper than 1.2 metres on this project unless the sides are battered back to a safe angle or a written assessment by the temporary works coordinator justifies it.

Support options include:

- Battering the sides to a stable angle, typically no steeper

the sides to a stable angle, typically no steeper than the natural angle of repose of the soil.
- Trench boxes and drag boxes, installed and moved so that workers are never in an unsupported length.
- Proprietary hydraulic shoring, installed from outside the trench wherever the system allows.
- Sheet piling for deep, long-duration, or waterlogged excavations, designed by the temporary works designer.

Excavations must be inspected by a competent person before each shift, after rain, after any

nt person before each shift, after rain, after any collapse or near miss, and after any change in loading nearby. Inspection results are recorded in the excavation register.

## Spoil, Materials, and Plant Near Edges

Spoil heaps and stored materials must be set back from the excavation edge by at least the depth of the excavation, and in no case closer than 1 metre, so that the surcharge does not destabilise the sides and material cannot roll into the trench. Vehicle stop blocks must be

roll into the trench. Vehicle stop blocks must be positioned where tipping or delivery vehicles approach an edge. Excavators must slew and travel so that tracks stay outside the zone of influence defined by the temporary works coordinator.

## Access, Egress, and Edge Protection

Safe access into any excavation deeper than 1 metre must be by secured ladder, with a ladder positioned so that no worker is ever more than 15 metres from a means of escape along the trench. Excavations in or near

of escape along the trench. Excavations in or near pedestrian areas must be protected by fixed barriers, not tape alone, and lit during darkness. Crossing points over trenches must be purpose-made walkways with guardrails on both sides.

## Groundwater and Confined Space Risks

Where groundwater is present, the dewatering method must be agreed with the temporary works coordinator, and pumps must discharge through settlement tanks before water leaves the site. Rising water in an excavation is an

aves the site. Rising water in an excavation is an evacuation trigger, not a nuisance: leave the excavation and report it.

Deep excavations, manholes, and chambers can accumulate hazardous atmospheres and may be confined spaces. Entry into any chamber or shaft requires atmosphere testing before entry and continuous monitoring, a top person in constant communication, and rescue equipment at the point of entry. Petrol or diesel plant must never run inside or at the edge of a confined excavation

run inside or at the edge of a confined excavation where exhaust can accumulate.

## Backfilling and Reinstatement

Backfill must be placed in compacted layers to the specification for the location, with support systems withdrawn progressively as filling proceeds, never removed entirely in advance of the fill. Marker tape must be laid above reinstated services at the depths given in the services specification, and the as-built position of any new or exposed service must be recorded and passed

new or exposed service must be recorded and passed to the section engineer for the site services drawings.

---

# Fire Safety and Hot Works

## Fire Risk on Construction Sites

Construction sites present elevated fire risk because temporary buildings, stored materials, and hot work coincide with incomplete fire compartments and detection systems. The site fire plan, displayed in the site office and at each fire point, shows escape routes, fire point locations, assembly points, and the location of the temporary fire alarm call points. The fire plan is updated whenever site layout changes affect escape

updated whenever site layout changes affect escape routes.

## Hot Work Permits

Hot work is any work involving flame, sparks, or heat sources capable of igniting materials: welding, flame cutting, grinding, soldering, bitumen boilers, and heat guns. All hot work outside the designated hot work bay requires a hot work permit issued by the site supervisor for that shift only.

The permit process requires:

1. Removal of combustible materials within 10 metres of the work, or protection with fire

hin 10 metres of the work, or protection with fire blankets where removal is impracticable.
2. A suitable fire extinguisher at the workface, checked and in date, with the operative trained to use it.
3. Screening of sparks with fire-retardant sheeting where other trades or the public are nearby.
4. A fire watch maintained during the work and for at least 60 minutes after the last hot work of the shift, with the watch period recorded on the permit before it is closed.
5. Hot work must stop at

rmit before it is closed.
5. Hot work must stop at least one hour before the site closes so the fire watch can be completed.

Permits must be returned to the site office and closed at the end of the task. An open permit found after the shift is treated as a near miss and investigated.

## Extinguishers and Fire Points

Fire points are positioned so that no workface is more than 30 metres from the nearest point. Each fire point carries a rotary alarm or air horn, signage, and extinguishers

tary alarm or air horn, signage, and extinguishers appropriate to the surrounding risk:

- Water or foam extinguishers for general combustible materials such as timber and packaging.
- Carbon dioxide extinguishers for electrical equipment and live panels.
- Dry powder extinguishers near fuel storage and plant refuelling areas.

Extinguishers must not be removed from fire points to the workface except under a hot work permit, and must be returned or replaced immediately after use. Discharged or

d or replaced immediately after use. Discharged or damaged extinguishers must be reported to the site office the same day. Fire points are inspected weekly and the inspection recorded.

Only attempt to fight a fire if it is smaller than a waste-paper bin, you have a clear escape route behind you, and the alarm has already been raised. Otherwise evacuate immediately.

## Storage of Flammable Materials

Flammable liquids, gas cylinders, and other highly combustible materials must be stored in the

highly combustible materials must be stored in the designated external compounds, never inside the building under construction.

- Store flammable liquids in a ventilated, bunded, lockable store at least 10 metres from site accommodation and boundaries. Keep quantities at the workface to the minimum needed for the shift, in approved safety containers.
- Store gas cylinders upright, secured against falling, in a caged external compound with full and empty cylinders segregated. Close cylinder

ull and empty cylinders segregated. Close cylinder valves when not in use and at the end of every shift.
- Keep oxygen cylinders at least 3 metres from fuel gas cylinders in storage, or separated by a fire-resistant partition.
- Site LPG heaters must be fed from cylinders located outside the space being heated, with hoses inspected daily.

Waste that can self-heat, such as oily rags and used sealant cloths, must be placed in closed metal bins and removed from the building daily.

## Temporary

and removed from the building daily.

## Temporary Accommodation and Escape Routes

Site cabins must be separated from the building under construction, from each other where practicable, and from flammable stores by the distances given in the fire plan. Escape routes within the building must be kept clear, lit where natural light is inadequate, and signed with photoluminescent signage as the structure progresses. Fire doors in completed sections must not be wedged open. Smoking is permitted

ions must not be wedged open. Smoking is permitted only in the designated smoking shelter.

---

# General Site Safety Guidance

## Purpose and Scope

This document provides general safety guidance for personnel working on construction sites managed under the Riverside Development programme. It covers personal protective equipment, site access, housekeeping, and communication of hazards. It applies to all workers, visitors, and delivery drivers entering the site boundary.

## Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

All personnel entering the working area must wear the mandatory minimum PPE at

orking area must wear the mandatory minimum PPE at all times:

- Safety helmet with chin strap in good condition, replaced at least every three years or after any significant impact.
- High-visibility vest or jacket, kept clean enough to remain conspicuous.
- Safety boots with steel or composite toe caps and midsole protection.

Additional task-specific PPE is required for certain activities. Eye protection must be worn when cutting, grinding, or drilling. Hearing protection is required in

ng, or drilling. Hearing protection is required in designated high-noise zones, indicated by blue signage, and whenever plant operates within 5 metres. Gloves appropriate to the task must be selected using the glove selection chart displayed in the site office.

Damaged PPE must be reported to the site supervisor and exchanged before work continues. PPE must not be modified or painted, as this can conceal damage or reduce protective performance.

## Site Access and Sign-In

All personnel must

e.

## Site Access and Sign-In

All personnel must sign in at the site office before starting work and sign out when leaving. The sign-in record is used for roll call in an emergency, so it must be accurate at all times.

First-time visitors must complete the site induction before unescorted access is permitted. The induction covers emergency arrangements, traffic routes, welfare facilities, and permit requirements. Inductions are valid for twelve months on this site.

Pedestrian routes are

twelve months on this site.

Pedestrian routes are separated from vehicle routes by physical barriers wherever practicable. Where routes cross, marked crossing points with mirrors or banksman control are provided. Never walk behind reversing plant. Make eye contact with the operator before approaching any machine.

## Housekeeping

Good housekeeping is the single most effective control against slips, trips, and falls on this site.

- Keep walkways and stairs clear of materials, cables, and

alkways and stairs clear of materials, cables, and debris at all times.
- Store materials in designated laydown areas, stacked stably and no higher than shoulder height unless racked.
- Route temporary cables overhead or through protective ramps, never loose across walkways.
- Dispose of waste in the segregated skips provided; do not allow waste to accumulate at the workface.
- Clean up spills immediately and use warning signs until the area is dry.

Each trade is responsible for clearing its

s dry.

Each trade is responsible for clearing its own work area at the end of every shift. The site supervisor conducts a daily housekeeping walk and records findings in the site diary.

## Hazard Reporting

Any worker who identifies a hazard must make the area safe if it is safe to do so, warn others nearby, and report the hazard to the site supervisor without delay. Hazard report cards are available at the site office and can be submitted anonymously.

Near misses must be reported in the

anonymously.

Near misses must be reported in the same way as accidents. Near-miss data is reviewed weekly to identify trends before they result in injury.

## Emergency Arrangements

The emergency assembly point is the car park adjacent to the main gate. On hearing the continuous alarm, stop work, isolate equipment if safe to do so, and walk to the assembly point by the nearest safe route. Do not use hoists during an evacuation. The fire marshal takes the roll call using the sign-in record.

hal takes the roll call using the sign-in record.

First aid boxes are located in the site office and the ground-floor welfare unit. The names of trained first aiders are displayed on the safety noticeboard.

# Working at Height

## General Requirements

Work at height means work in any place where a person could fall a distance liable to cause personal injury. This includes work on ladders, scaffolds, roofs, and near excavations or floor openings. Work at height must be avoided where reasonably practicable; where it cannot be avoided, work must be planned, supervised, and carried out using equipment that prevents falls or minimises the distance and consequences of a fall.

A working-at-height plan

consequences of a fall.

A working-at-height plan must be prepared before any task above 2 metres, and for any task below 2 metres where a fall could still cause injury, such as work adjacent to protruding rebar or an open excavation.

## Ladders and Stepladders

Ladders may only be used for short-duration light work, generally up to 30 minutes at a position, where a risk assessment shows that more suitable equipment is not justified.

- Inspect ladders before each use for damaged stiles,

nspect ladders before each use for damaged stiles, rungs, and feet. Tag and remove damaged ladders from service.
- Set leaning ladders at a 75-degree angle, approximately one unit out for every four units up.
- Secure the ladder at the top, or where this is not possible, at the base; footing by a second person is a last resort.
- Extend the ladder at least 1 metre above the landing point where it is used for access.
- Maintain three points of contact when climbing. Do not carry heavy or bulky

contact when climbing. Do not carry heavy or bulky loads up a ladder; use a tool belt or hoist line.
- Do not stand on the top three rungs of a leaning ladder or the top two steps of a stepladder.

Class 1 industrial or EN 131 professional ladders are the only classes permitted on site. Domestic-class ladders must not be brought onto site.

## Scaffolding

Scaffolds must be erected, altered, and dismantled only by trained and competent scaffolders under a handover procedure. Incomplete

scaffolders under a handover procedure. Incomplete scaffolds must display a prominent "Scaffold Incomplete – Do Not Use" sign.

Every working platform must have:

- Double guardrails, with the top rail at least 950 mm above the platform and no gap exceeding 470 mm.
- Toe boards at least 150 mm high on all open edges.
- Fully boarded decks with boards secured against uplift and displacement.
- Safe ladder access within the scaffold bay, with the ladder gate or hatch kept closed when not in use.

ladder gate or hatch kept closed when not in use.

Scaffolds must be inspected by a competent person before first use, after any alteration or adverse weather, and at intervals not exceeding seven days. Inspection records are kept in the scaffold register in the site office. Never remove ties, boards, or guardrails; request an alteration through the scaffolding contractor.

## Mobile Towers

Mobile access towers must be assembled by operatives holding current tower training, following the

ives holding current tower training, following the manufacturer's instruction manual. Wheels must be locked before use, and the tower must never be moved with people or materials on the platform. The maximum platform height must not exceed the manufacturer's stated ratio to base dimensions, or the tower must be tied or ballasted.

## Edge Protection and Openings

All floor edges, roof edges, and openings through which a person could fall must be protected with fixed edge protection: double

st be protected with fixed edge protection: double guardrails and toe boards, or full covers secured against displacement and marked "Opening – Do Not Remove".

Where fixed protection is not practicable for short-duration work, a personal fall protection system may be used. Fall restraint, which prevents the user from reaching the edge, is preferred over fall arrest. Anyone using a harness must be trained, and harnesses must be inspected before each use and formally examined every six months. A

each use and formally examined every six months. A rescue plan must be in place before any fall-arrest work begins; suspension in a harness after a fall can cause serious harm within minutes.

## Fragile Surfaces

Fragile roof surfaces, including rooflights and fibre cement sheets, must be identified before roof work starts. Work on or near fragile surfaces requires crawling boards or staging that spans the supports, together with edge protection or fall arrest. Warning signs must be fixed at

ion or fall arrest. Warning signs must be fixed at every access point to a fragile roof.
